ui_settings_metric_help_desc: Display info icons next to metrics
ui_settings_auto_session_names_desc: Use time of day and sport type to name sessions
ui_settings_compact_layout_desc: Single-column layout and shift content right to reveal more of the map
ui_settings_map_pitch_desc: Camera pitch angle when viewing the map
ui_settings_about_desc: PaceVault is a local-first fitness activity analyzer. All your data stays on your device — nothing is sent to any server. If you encounter a bug or have a feature request, head over to GitHub.
ui_settings_about_github_desc: Found a bug or have a feature request? Open an issue
ui_settings_about_sources_desc: Bibliography for all engine formulas and algorithms
ui_thresholds_subtitle: Your heart rate and power baselines
ui_thresholds_max_hr_greater: Max HR must be greater than resting HR
ui_thresholds_rest_hr_less: Resting HR must be less than max HR
ui_data_storage_desc: Your FIT files and session data — stored locally in your browser
ui_data_reimport_desc: Re-parse stored FIT files to recalculate TSS, zones, and personal bests
ui_data_delete_all_desc: Permanently remove all sessions, records, and your profile
ui_delete_dialog_desc: This will permanently delete everything: all training sessions, personal bests, lap data, time-series records, and your profile (name, thresholds, preferences). This action cannot be undone.
ui_pace_est_desc: Enter a recent race result to calculate your threshold pace using the Daniels VDOT formula.
ui_onboarding_welcome_desc: Track your training load, spot personal bests, and analyze every session — all right here in your browser.
ui_onboarding_your_data_desc: Import .FIT files from your Garmin, Wahoo, or other device.
ui_onboarding_testdata_desc: Explore the app with ~70 generated sessions for an experienced athlete.
ui_session_rename_desc: Enter a new name for this session.
ui_session_delete_desc: Are you sure you want to delete the {sport} session from {date}? This action cannot be undone.
ui_coach_threshold_desc: To generate training plans, set your threshold pace in Settings. This is your pace at lactate threshold — roughly your 1-hour race pace.
ui_coach_predictor_subtitle: Predict race times from a recent result using VDOT
ui_coach_converter_subtitle: Convert between pace, distance, and time
engine_te_sum_too_easy: Too easy to produce a meaningful training stimulus.
engine_te_sum_extreme: Extreme session — high aerobic and anaerobic impact.
engine_te_sum_steady_aerobic: Steady aerobic session with minimal anaerobic demand.
engine_te_sum_high_intensity: High-intensity session with strong anaerobic stimulus.
engine_te_sum_mixed_intensity: Mixed-intensity session with significant aerobic and anaerobic stimulus.
engine_te_sum_easy_maintenance: Easy maintenance session — light aerobic stimulus.
engine_te_sum_moderate: Moderate aerobic session with some anaerobic contribution.
engine_te_sum_light: Light session — minimal overall training effect.
coach_form_overload: Deep Fatigue. High Risk. Rest recommended.
coach_immature_detraining: Your fitness metrics are still stabilizing (less than 4 weeks of data). Current readings suggest training has been light recently. Focus on building consistency — the numbers will become more reliable as your history grows.
coach_immature_fresh: Your fitness metrics are still stabilizing (less than 4 weeks of data). You appear well-rested based on early readings. Keep training consistently and the picture will sharpen over the coming weeks.
coach_immature_neutral: Your fitness metrics are still stabilizing (less than 4 weeks of data). Your training load looks balanced so far. Keep logging sessions — reliable coaching insights need about 4 weeks of history.
coach_immature_optimal: Your fitness metrics are still stabilizing (less than 4 weeks of data). Early signs suggest a solid training load. Stay consistent and monitor how you feel — the metrics will become more meaningful soon.
coach_immature_overload: Your fitness metrics are still stabilizing (less than 4 weeks of data). Early readings suggest high fatigue relative to your short history. Consider an easy day, but keep in mind these numbers are preliminary.
coach_undertraining_detraining: Your fitness is declining and your recent training load is well below your long-term average. You are at risk of deconditioning. Gradually ramp volume back up to arrest the fitness loss.
coach_undertraining_fresh: You are well-rested, but your recent training load is significantly below your baseline. While you are ready to perform now, sustained underloading will erode your fitness. Consider adding volume.
coach_undertraining_neutral: Your readiness is balanced, but your weekly load has dropped well below your chronic average. Maintaining this level too long leads to deconditioning. A moderate increase would keep fitness on track.
coach_undertraining_optimal: You have been training hard recently, yet your acute load is still below your long-term average. This can happen after a sudden taper. Ensure the reduced load is intentional and time-limited.
coach_undertraining_overload: You are carrying significant fatigue despite a low recent training load relative to your history. This may indicate accumulated stress from non-training factors. Prioritize recovery before adding volume.
coach_sweet_detraining: Your fitness is starting to decline because training has been too light recently. Your body has fully recovered and is ready for more stimulus. Consider gradually increasing your training volume to get back on track.
coach_sweet_fresh: You are well-rested and your fitness is high relative to your fatigue. This is the ideal state for racing or key workouts. If you have a target event coming up, now is the time to perform.
coach_sweet_neutral: Your training load is balanced with your recovery. This is normal day-to-day training territory. Keep following your plan and focus on aerobic development and consistency.
coach_sweet_optimal: You have been training hard recently and your body is adapting. This is a good place to be — your fitness is growing. Just make sure you are sleeping well and eating enough to support recovery.
coach_sweet_overload: You are carrying significant fatigue from recent training. Your body needs rest to absorb the training stress and come back stronger. Take easy days or a full rest day before pushing hard again.
coach_risk_mod_detraining: Your fitness is declining, and your training ramp rate is elevated. Avoid jumping back in too aggressively — a moderate, progressive return to training is safer than a sudden spike.
coach_risk_mod_fresh: You are well-rested, but your training has ramped up faster than usual. You can still perform well, but monitor for early signs of strain. Avoid stacking another big increase this week.
coach_risk_mod_neutral: Your readiness is balanced, but your training ramp rate is above the safe zone. Hold steady or slightly reduce this week to let your body catch up before pushing further.
coach_risk_mod_optimal: You are in a productive training phase, and your load ramp is moderately elevated. This is a fine line — keep pushing, but do not stack another big increase on top. Listen to your body.
coach_risk_mod_overload: You are carrying deep fatigue and your load ramp is elevated. Your body is under significant stress. Easy days or complete rest are strongly recommended before resuming hard training.
coach_risk_high_detraining: Your fitness is declining and your recent load spike is in the high-risk zone. Back off immediately. Rebuild volume gradually over 2-3 weeks rather than trying to make up for lost time.
coach_risk_high_fresh: You are rested, but your load has spiked sharply — putting you at elevated injury risk. Scale back intensity or volume this week. Being fresh does not protect against a load spike.
coach_risk_high_neutral: Your readiness is balanced, but your load has increased dangerously fast. Reduce volume or intensity for the next few days. A sharp ramp at any readiness level raises injury risk.
coach_risk_high_optimal: You are training hard and your load has spiked into the danger zone. The combination of high fatigue and rapid ramp-up is the highest-risk scenario. Take a recovery day now.
coach_risk_high_overload: You are deeply fatigued with a dangerous load spike. This is the highest-risk state — injury likelihood is significantly elevated. Rest immediately and do not resume hard training until fatigue subsides.
coach_no_data: Not enough data yet. Upload sessions to get recommendations.
coach_rationale_no_data: No training history. Starting with a conservative plan of easy runs.
coach_rationale_taper: Taper plan for race on {raceDate}. {daysToRace} days to race.
coach_rationale_base: TSB is {tsb}. Form: {formStatus}. ACWR {acwr} ({risk} injury risk).
coach_rationale_immature: {base} Metrics are still stabilizing ({days} days of data) — plan is conservative until 4 weeks of history.
exp_tss_oneLiner: Measures how hard a workout was relative to your personal threshold.
exp_tss_analogy: Think of TSS like a workout cost in energy credits. A 100-credit workout is a solid effort; 200+ means you wrote a big check your body needs time to cash.
exp_tss_range: Under 150: low stress, quick recovery. 150-300: moderate, some residual fatigue. 300-450: high, requires 2+ days recovery. 450+: very high, extended recovery needed.
exp_trimp_oneLiner: Estimates workout stress from heart rate data using the Banister formula.
exp_trimp_analogy: If TSS is your workout price tag from a power meter, TRIMP is the price tag from your heart rate monitor. Both measure cost, just using different currencies.
exp_trimp_range: Normalized to TSS scale: ~100 for a 1-hour threshold effort. Compare with TSS ranges for interpretation.
exp_duration_oneLiner: A rough stress estimate based only on session duration when no power or heart rate data is available.
exp_duration_analogy: Think of it as a minimum charge on your training credit card — the app logs that you trained, even if it cannot measure how hard.
exp_duration_range: Fixed at 30 TSS per hour of activity.
exp_ctl_oneLiner: Your fitness level built up over the past ~6 weeks of training.
exp_ctl_analogy: CTL is like your savings account balance. Each workout is a deposit. The balance grows slowly with consistent contributions and shrinks gradually if you stop depositing.
exp_ctl_range: Higher = more fit. Typical: 40-70 recreational, 70-100 competitive amateur, 100+ elite. Takes 4-6 weeks of data to stabilize.
exp_atl_oneLiner: How much training stress you have accumulated over the past ~week.
exp_atl_analogy: ATL is like your credit card bill — it reflects your recent spending (training). A big week of training runs up the bill fast. A rest week lets it settle back down.
exp_atl_range: Relative to CTL: ATL significantly above CTL means high fatigue. ATL near or below CTL means you are well-recovered.
exp_tsb_oneLiner: How fresh or fatigued you are right now — positive means rested, negative means tired.
exp_tsb_analogy: TSB is your energy balance. Positive means you have reserves in the tank. Negative means you are running on credit. A little bit of credit is fine (that is how you build fitness), but too much debt leads to burnout.
exp_tsb_range: Above +25: detraining risk. +5 to +25: fresh, race-ready. -10 to +5: normal training. -30 to -10: productive overload. Below -30: high fatigue, rest recommended.
exp_acwr_oneLiner: Compares your recent training load to your long-term average — tells you if you are ramping up safely.
exp_acwr_analogy: Think of ACWR like a speedometer for your training ramp rate. Cruising at 0.8-1.3 is safe highway speed. Above 1.5, you are speeding and risk a crash (injury). Below 0.8, you are coasting and losing momentum.
exp_acwr_range: Below 0.8: undertraining, losing fitness. 0.8-1.3: sweet spot, safe progression. 1.3-1.5: caution, elevated risk. Above 1.5: danger zone, high injury risk.
exp_normalizedPower_oneLiner: The true intensity of your ride, accounting for the extra cost of surging and recovering.
exp_normalizedPower_analogy: Average power is like your average driving speed on a trip. NP is more like how much fuel you actually burned. Stop-and-go traffic uses more fuel than cruising at the same average speed.
exp_normalizedPower_range: Compare to your FTP. NP close to FTP means a threshold-level effort. NP at 75% of FTP is endurance pace. Above FTP for a sustained ride is very hard.
exp_gradeAdjustedPace_oneLiner: Your equivalent flat-ground pace, adjusted for hills using metabolic cost modeling.
exp_gradeAdjustedPace_analogy: GAP is like adjusting your driving speed for road conditions. Going 60 km/h uphill in 3rd gear uses the same engine effort as 90 km/h on flat in 5th. GAP shows the flat road equivalent of your effort.
exp_gradeAdjustedPace_range: Interpreted as pace (min/km). GAP faster than actual pace = uphill effort. GAP slower than actual pace = downhill coasting.
exp_recovery_oneLiner: How quickly your heart rate drops between work intervals.
exp_recovery_analogy: Think of your heart like an engine cooling down after a hard acceleration. A fit engine returns to idle quickly. A deconditioned engine stays revved up longer.
exp_recovery_range: Above 25 bpm: strong recovery, well-adapted cardiovascular system. 15-25 bpm: adequate recovery, typical for moderately trained athletes. Below 15 bpm: slow recovery, may indicate fatigue, dehydration, or room for aerobic base work.
exp_pacingTrend_oneLiner: Whether your pace held steady, faded, or built across laps.
exp_pacingTrend_analogy: Pacing trend is like monitoring your fuel gauge during a road trip. Even fuel consumption (stable) means you planned well. Running out early (fading) means you started too fast. Having reserves at the end (building) means you were conservative — or executed a perfect negative split.
exp_pacingTrend_range: Beyond +3%: fading — pace slowed across laps, typical fatigue pattern. -3% to +3%: stable — consistent pacing, well-executed effort. Beyond -3%: building — pace improved, negative split strategy.
exp_trainingZones_oneLiner: Pace-based intensity zones derived from your threshold pace.
exp_trainingZones_analogy: Zones are like gears on a bicycle. Each gear is suited to different terrain and effort. You would not climb a hill in top gear or cruise the flats in first. Training zones help you pick the right gear for each workout's goal.
exp_trainingZones_range: Zone 1: Recovery. Zone 2: Aerobic/easy. Zone 3: Tempo. Zone 4: Threshold. Zone 5: VO2max and above. Each zone is defined as a pace range relative to your threshold.
exp_aerobicTE_oneLiner: How much this session improved your aerobic endurance, on a 0–5 scale.
exp_aerobicTE_analogy: Think of Aerobic TE like a receipt for your workout. A score of 2 means you kept the lights on (maintaining fitness). A score of 4 means you made a serious deposit in your endurance bank account.
exp_aerobicTE_range: 0–0.9: No effect. 1–1.9: Minor benefit. 2–2.9: Maintaining fitness. 3–3.9: Improving fitness. 4–4.9: Highly improving. 5.0: Overreaching.
exp_anaerobicTE_oneLiner: How much this session stressed your anaerobic (high-intensity) energy system, on a 0–5 scale.
exp_anaerobicTE_analogy: Anaerobic TE is like measuring how many times you redlined your engine during a drive. A low score means you cruised. A high score means you spent serious time in the red zone.
exp_anaerobicTE_range: 0–0.9: No anaerobic stimulus. 1–1.9: Minor. 2–2.9: Moderate high-intensity work. 3–3.9: Significant VO2max stimulus. 4–4.9: Very high anaerobic stress. 5.0: Overreaching.
exp_avgHr_oneLiner: The mean heart rate across the entire session, reflecting overall cardiovascular effort.
exp_avgHr_analogy: Average HR is like the average speed on your car's trip computer — it smooths out all the stops and sprints into one number that summarizes the whole journey.
exp_avgHr_range: Zone 1–2 (easy): 50–70% of max HR. Zone 3 (tempo): 70–80%. Zone 4 (threshold): 80–90%. Zone 5 (VO2max): 90–100%. Actual values depend on individual max HR.
exp_avgPace_oneLiner: Your average time per kilometer, the primary intensity metric for running and swimming.
exp_avgPace_analogy: Pace is like the price per kilogram at the grocery store — it normalizes the cost (time) by quantity (distance) so you can compare regardless of how much you bought.
exp_avgPace_range: Highly individual. Recreational runners: 6:00–7:30/km. Competitive amateurs: 4:30–6:00/km. Elite: sub-3:30/km. Compare to your own history and threshold pace.
exp_avgSpeed_oneLiner: Your average speed in km/h, the primary intensity metric for cycling.
exp_avgSpeed_analogy: Speed is the cyclist's speedometer reading averaged over the whole ride. It answers the simple question: how fast did I go?
exp_avgSpeed_range: Recreational cycling: 20–25 km/h. Club riders: 25–32 km/h. Competitive: 32–40 km/h. Elite TT: 45+ km/h. Highly affected by terrain and wind.
exp_avgPower_oneLiner: The mean power output across the session, measuring raw mechanical work.
exp_avgPower_analogy: If Normalized Power is your fuel bill, Average Power is the odometer reading — it tells you what happened mechanically, without accounting for the extra cost of stop-and-go.
exp_avgPower_range: Compare to your FTP. Endurance rides: 55–75% FTP. Tempo: 76–90%. Threshold: 91–105%. Above threshold for sustained rides is very hard.
exp_elevation_oneLiner: Total meters climbed and descended during the session.
exp_elevation_analogy: Elevation gain is like counting flights of stairs — two 10km runs can feel completely different if one climbs 500m and the other is flat.
exp_elevation_range: Flat: under 100m gain. Rolling: 100–300m. Hilly: 300–700m. Mountainous: 700m+. All relative to session distance.
exp_cadence_oneLiner: Your average steps or revolutions per minute during the session.
exp_cadence_analogy: Cadence is like the RPM gauge in a car. Too low and you are lugging the engine; too high and you are over-revving. The sweet spot depends on the conditions and your engine.
exp_cadence_range: Running: 160–180+ spm (elite often 180+). Cycling: 80–100 rpm (varies by terrain and effort). Lower cadence = more muscular load, higher cadence = more cardiovascular load.
exp_vdot_oneLiner: A single number that estimates your current running fitness based on a recent race result.
exp_vdot_analogy: VDOT is like a credit score for your running — one number that summarizes a bunch of underlying factors into a quick snapshot of where you stand.
exp_vdot_range: Beginner: 25–35. Recreational: 35–45. Competitive: 45–55. Elite: 55–70+. World class: 75–85.
ui_reimport_dialog_desc: This re-parses all stored FIT files using your current thresholds. TSS, zones, laps, and personal bests get recalculated. Existing session data is overwritten.
ui_metrics_chart_subtitle: Long-term fitness, fatigue, and readiness trends
ui_splits_desc: Custom splits divide the route into equal-distance segments. Device uses the laps recorded by your watch or bike computer.
ui_pb_subtitle: New records achieved in this session
ui_zone_color_desc: Colors the route on the map by training zone
toast_invalid_file_desc: {fileName} is not a supported file type
toast_save_failed_desc: Could not save sessions to database
toast_reimport_no_profile_desc: Set up your thresholds before reimporting
toast_reimport_no_files_desc: No stored FIT files to reimport
ui_demo_banner_text: You're exploring PaceVault with test data
